request.question: Ex Mach Tina was the 8th episode of the series created by whom?

title: Bob's Burgers
Beneath (The Secret Circle)
Kelly Johnson (engineer)
A Reptile Dysfunction
Ex Mach Tina
The Son Also Rises (Grimm)
Flashes Before Your Eyes
The Van Buren Boys
Bye Bye Boo Boo
Elegy for a Pig
sentences: Bob's Burgers is an American adult animated sitcom created by Loren Bouchard that premiered on Fox on January 9, 2011.
The series centers on the Belchers – parents Bob and Linda, and their children Tina, Gene, and Louise – who run a hamburger restaurant.
The show was conceived by Bouchard after he developed "Home Movies".
It is produced and distributed in association with 20th Century Fox Television.
"Beneath" is the 8th episode of the first season of the CW television series "The Secret Circle", and the series' 8th episode overall.
It was aired on November 3, 2011.
The episode was written by Don Whitehead & Holly Henderson and it was directed by John Fawcett.
Clarence Leonard "Kelly" Johnson (February 27, 1910 – December 21, 1990) was an American aeronautical and systems engineer.
He is recognized for his contributions to a series of important aircraft designs, most notably the Lockheed U-2 and SR-71 Blackbird.

Besides the first production aircraft to exceed Mach 3, he also produced the first fighter capable of Mach 2, the United States' first operational jet fighter, as well as the first U.S. fighter to exceed 400 mph, and many other contributions to a large number of aircraft.
As a member and first team leader of the Lockheed Skunk Works, Johnson worked for more than four decades and is said to have been an "organizing genius".

He played a leading role in the design of over forty aircraft, including several honored with the prestigious Collier Trophy, acquiring a reputation as one of the most talented and prolific aircraft design engineers in the history of aviation.

In 2003, as part of its commemoration of the 100th anniversary of the Wright Brothers' flight, "Aviation Week & Space Technology" ranked Johnson 8th on its list of the top 100 "most important, most interesting, and most influential people" in the first century of aerospace.
Hall Hibbard, Johnson's Lockheed boss, referring to Johnson's Swedish ancestry once remarked to Ben Rich: "That damned Swede can actually see air."
"A Reptile Dysfunction" is the 8th episode of season 5 of the supernatural drama television series "Grimm" and the 96th episode overall, which premiered on February 5, 2016, on the cable network NBC.
The episode was written by Michael Golamco and was directed by David Straiton.

In the episode, Nick meets Meisner and Trubel for information about Hadrian's Wall and the purpose of the organization while also investigating a murder in a lake, which is rumored to be the place for a monster living in the lake.
"Ex Mach Tina" is the 8th episode of the seventh season of the American animated comedy series "Bob's Burgers" and the 115th episode overall.
It was written by Greg Thompson and directed by Brian Loschiavo.

Its guest stars are David Herman as Mr. Frond and as Mr. Branca, Robert Smigel as the school's night security guard, Samantha Bee as school nurse Liz, Jenny Slate as Tammy Larsen and Bobby Tisdale as Zeke.
It originally aired in the US on FOX Network at January 8, 2017.
In this episode Tina hurts her ankle and uses a telecommunication robot for school, so she can stay at home.
Jimmy Jr. develops a relationship to the robot version of Tina.
Meanwhile Bob buys a banjo and waits for a good moment to play it.
"The Son Also Rises" is the 8th episode of season 6 of the supernatural drama television series "Grimm" and the 118th episode overall, which premiered on February 24, 2017, on the cable network NBC.
The episode was written by Todd Milliner and Nick Peet and was directed by Peter Werner.
In the episode, Eve is attacked from the other side of the mirror and Nick decides to stay with her.
Meanwhile, Hank and Wu investigate the murders of scientists, discovering that the murders are related to an experiment to resurrect a man's son.
"Flashes Before Your Eyes" is the 8th episode of the third season of the American drama television series "Lost", and the show's 57th episode overall.
The episode was written by the series co-creator, show runner and executive producer Damon Lindelof and supervising producer Drew Goddard, and directed by Jack Bender.
It first aired in the United States on February 14, 2007, on the American Broadcasting Company.
The episode received mostly positive reviews from critics.
Lindelof and Goddard were nominated for the Writers Guild of America (WGA) Award for Best Episodic Drama at the February 2008 ceremony for writing the episode.
"The Van Buren Boys" is the 148th episode of the sitcom "Seinfeld", and name of a fictional New York street gang.
Their sign is one hand with all the fingers and thumb 'up' and spread out.
The other hand has all but the thumb and pointer finger up, for a total of eight (8).
The gang is named for the 8th President of the United States, Martin Van Buren (who was the first president from New York).
This was the 14th episode for the 8th season.
It aired on February 6, 1997.
"Bye Bye Boo Boo" is the 16th episode of the sixth season of the animated comedy series "Bob's Burgers" and the overall 104th episode, and is written by Scott Jacobson and directed by Tyree Dillihay.
It aired on Fox in the United States on May 8, 2016.
In the episode, after hearing that Boo Boo is leaving Boyz 4 Now, Louise enters a contest that could finally give Tina a chance to meet him.
Meanwhile, Bob and Linda learn about a mobster who got shot in the restaurant in 1931, which causes tension between Bob and Jimmy Pesto.
"Log 105: Elegy for a Pig" (a.k.a. simply "Elegy for a Pig") is the 8th episode of the 3rd season of the American television series "Adam-12", the 60th episode of the series overall.
Originally aired on November 21, 1970, the episode focuses on the life of a recently killed police officer.

The episode's format is a major departure from the series' usual format; instead of presenting a day in the life of two officers, it presents a first-person narration from the point of view of one of the series' main protagonists.
It is presented as a tribute to police officers who have been killed in the line of duty.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Ex Mach Tina was the 8th episode of the series created by whom?
hop1_docs: Ex Mach Tina | "Ex Mach Tina" is the 8th episode of the seventh season of the American animated comedy series "Bob's Burgers" and the 115th episode overall.  It was written by Greg Thompson and directed by Brian Loschiavo.  Its guest stars are David Herman as Mr. Frond and as Mr. Branca, Robert Smigel as the school's night security guard, Samantha Bee as school nurse Liz, Jenny Slate as Tammy Larsen and Bobby Tisdale as Zeke.  It originally aired in the US on FOX Network at January 8, 2017.  In this episode Tina hurts her ankle and uses a telecommunication robot for school, so she can stay at home.  Jimmy Jr. develops a relationship to the robot version of Tina.  Meanwhile Bob buys a banjo and waits for a good moment to play it.

Mach a Šebestová | Mach a Šebestová (in English: Mach and Šebestová or Max and Sally) is a Czech cartoon series.  It was created by writer Miloš Macourek, director Jaroslav Doubrava, and animator Adolf Born.  The animated characters were voiced by Petr Nárožný.  The story follows the adventures of two third-year pupils and a torn off telephone receiver.  The first series of 13 episodes was broadcast on Czechoslovak Television in 1982.  Mach a Šebestová is one of the most popular serials made for children's television programme Večerníček.  The cartoon was adapted for the film "Mach, Šebestová a kouzelné sluchátko" (Max, Sally and the Magic Phone, 2001) by director Václav Vorlíček.  In 2010, "Theatre Lampion" presented a stage adaptation of the series in the Czech National theatre.

List of Trailer Park Boys episodes | "Trailer Park Boys" is a Canadian mockumentary television series created and directed by Mike Clattenburg and a continuation of Clattenburg's 1999 film of the same name.  The series focuses on the misadventures of a group of trailer park residents, some of whom are ex-convicts, living in the fictional Sunnyvale Trailer Park in Dartmouth, Nova Scotia.

The Equestranauts | "The Equestranauts" is the 17th episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Bob's Burgers", and the 62nd episode overall.  It was written by Dan Mintz and directed by Tyree Dillihay, and was released on April 13, 2014.  Parodying the "" fanbase of the animated children's cartoon "", the episode's plot centers on Tina discovering her favorite animated pony cartoon has a fanbase of adult men, one of whom tricks Tina out of her rare toy pony, forcing Bob to go undercover and try to get it back.

List of Crazy Ex-Girlfriend episodes | "Crazy Ex-Girlfriend" is an American musical comedy-drama television series created by Rachel Bloom and Aline Brosh McKenna, that premiered on The CW on October 12, 2015.  The series stars Bloom as Rebecca Bunch, a depressed young woman who decides to follow her ex-boyfriend from New York City to West Covina, California in the hopes of finding real happiness.  All episode titles follow the pattern of referring to Rebecca's ex-boyfriend, Josh.  On March 11, 2016, The CW renewed the series for a second season which premiered on October 21, 2016.

Sea Me Now | "Sea Me Now" is the second episode of the seventh season of the animated comedy series "Bob's Burgers" and the overall 109th episode, and is written by Dan Fybel and directed by Chris Song.  It aired on Fox in the United States on October 9, 2016.  In the episode, Teddy tries to impress his ex-wife, Denise, when the Belchers accompany him for a ride on his newly restored boat.  Meanwhile, Tina takes care of Bob's restaurant eraser, in an attempt to get her own cell phone.

List of 30 Rock episodes | "30 Rock" is an American satirical television sitcom that ran on NBC from October 11, 2006, to January 31, 2013.  Created by Tina Fey, the series follows the lives of the head writer of "The Girlie Show with Tracy Jordan" (TGS), Liz Lemon (Tina Fey), the other staff members of "TGS", and their network executive, Jack Donaghy (Alec Baldwin).  A total of 138 episodes of "30 Rock" were produced and aired over seven seasons.
hop1_titles: List of Trailer Park Boys episodes

hop2_docs: Bob's Burgers | Bob's Burgers is an American adult animated sitcom created by Loren Bouchard that premiered on Fox on January 9, 2011.  The series centers on the Belchers – parents Bob and Linda, and their children Tina, Gene, and Louise – who run a hamburger restaurant.  The show was conceived by Bouchard after he developed "Home Movies".  It is produced and distributed in association with 20th Century Fox Television.

Burger Wars (Bob's Burgers) | "Burger Wars" is the tenth episode of the first season of the animated television series "Bob's Burgers".  The episode originally aired on the Fox network in the United States on April 10, 2011.

List of Bob's Burgers episodes | "Bob's Burgers" is an American animated television sitcom that premiered on the Fox network on January 9, 2011.  The series finished its seventh season on June 11, 2017.  It centers on the Belchers, a family consisting of parents Bob (H. Jon Benjamin) and Linda (John Roberts) and their children Tina (Dan Mintz), Gene (Eugene Mirman) and Louise (Kristen Schaal), who run a hamburger restaurant.  The show's premise originated with Loren Bouchard, who serves as an executive producer along with Jim Dauterive.  The show is said to fill the void created by the conclusion of "King of the Hill", which Dauterive worked on for nearly its entire run.
List of Bob's Burgers characters | The following is a list of characters from American animated television series "Bob's Burgers".
Bob's Burgers (season 8) | The eighth season of the animated comedy series "Bob's Burgers" began airing on Fox in the United States on October 1, 2017.

Bob's Burgers (season 4) | The fourth season of the animated comedy series "Bob's Burgers" began airing on Fox in the United States on September 29, 2013, and concluded on May 18, 2014.  The show was once again part of Fox's Sunday night lineup, initially airing in its usual timeslot of 8:30 PM Eastern.  Beginning on March 9 and continuing for the rest of the season, "Bob's Burgers" moved to 7:00 PM Eastern; the move came as a result of the addition of "" to the network's lineup.
Bob's Burgers (season 3) | The third season of the animated sitcom "Bob's Burgers" began airing on Fox in the United States on September 30, 2012, and concluded on May 12, 2013.
response.summary_2: Loren Bouchard. The series Bob's Burgers is an American adult animated sitcom created by Loren Bouchard.
